author: @AdrianP247
text:
When I was growing up in the 70's, our family home had horizontal Venetian blinds in our dining room and a ranch slider in the adjacent living room. When the ranchslider was open and the window in the dining room was open, sometimes there was a "cross-wind" going through. The Venetian blinds made a real racket vibrating from the wind passing through them.
I bet those blinds' vibration could have generated MEGAwatts!

author: @Nakasupy
text: Hi Robert. Greetings from Canada: When you were testing the car fan on the side of the wall in your previous video, I was very surprised when you didn't also try moving the fan to blow along the wall, which I believe offered the possibility for even greater energy output. As well, isn't it twice the odds of the wind blowing that way, than at right angles. Even at various angles, the lower efficiency, for potentially twice as often, might be more productive. It might be somewhat like the battles, and wars thing.🙂

replies.author: @donaldhanson2908
replies.text:
True. building design includes wind load calculations. and out in the weather, that air has to go some where.
Depending on the wind direction, that is usually along the face of the structure.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I just wanted to see if it did anything mate - that was all

replies.author: @Nakasupy
replies.text: Thanks for the reply, Robert. If you decide to try a wall mounted gen again, may I request you slowly rotate the "wind source" from 90° to the wall, to running parallel to it. It would be very interesting to see the effect that the various "wind" angles have on the output, in particular to the car fan attached to the CD Gen. It would also be neat to see the output from that black plastic open faced turbine fan that you had tested earlier, along the wall. One reason I liked it, is that it could likely be 3D printed. After all, the more excuses I can accumulate to buy one of those printing machines, the better. LOL. Thanks, again.

author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: A great way to recycle blinds!  I love the cone you put in the middle.  That is the type of cone I intend to use by itself for a rotor.  All I need to do is figure out the design of the narrow wedge shaped blades.  Pointy end at cone apex and spreading out like spokes.  The problem will be setting the ideal geometry of the radial spiral.  All it takes is to make just one vane to use as a template for the rest.  My 3D printer pen might be perfect for making that template.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Go for it! - was thinking of 3d printing but I wanted a way to do it without that so more folks could do it - but 3d printing is the way to go mate

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: If you had cut a plastic drain pipe in half like you did before, you could have stuck that round the Perspex and put a strengthener round the top like you did with the blind.  If you were mounting Horizontally then that is fine.  If you wanted to mount it on a wall, put a big cone in the middle to direct air to the edges (at least as big as the fins, bigger the better.  It should get up quite a few revs and even take a load on it.  Horizontal would need to be triad first, then fit a cone to a wall mounted one.  I would try it, but I have nothing to try it with.

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
The clear plexiglass make a nice demo, but any discarded bicycle wheel would work too.
You can also cut pvc pipe lengthwise if you  want something sturdier.  The blinds are good for <1ft (30cm), but pvc can do 10ft (~3m) ... steel pipe can do 20ft (6m)  The only issue is that past about 1m you need support for the top and the large ones can be difficult to stop even if you leave the disc brakes on the wheels.

You don't have to connect it to an electric motor either.  I helped a farmer buddy of mine  build a bank of the steel pipe ones to pump water for irrigation.  It might have even looked nice too if we had paint other than Kubota orange.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - but this design won't go over a few inches high

author: @michaeltucker8645
text: I could pick your brain all day lol. Sry so much commenting.  When ideas come they come in mass. You might even consider combining the idea of your vertical generator and this.  Using the style fan from the vertical fan and cage fans to increase the fan speed of rotation. You could possibly put several of those style blade side by side or even stack them all on 1 serpentine type belt  between them to a motor like pistons in a combustion motor to the crack shaft.  Also easily build  much longer  vertical style blade from your  slates and apply the side by side or stack method.  I bet that could produce some serious rpms on a normal wind active day.  That also would be fairly low profile.  Could literally be 1/4 the width of your vertical fans blade compensating lost of blade for length of the blades and number of individual blade linked together.  You could get aways with a wall generator that barely come 3 to 4 inches off the wall.  Again sry for the long winded comments.  My area I'm not the only person I know that loves this stuff and rarely can brain storm ideas to anyone who can even understand lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting stuff mate - thanks for posting

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Last one I promise what about using a Tesla turbine style method. Funnels to increase input winds . You talking about micro  wind generation on a flat wall surface. Could easily run inputs n outputs of dozens of small 12v dc motors  with that method. Even put strings in series  or parallel with different sized motors to reach the needed voltage and amp output of whatever your trying to run or charge. I gotta get to write this down on paper. Thanks again Robert for the massive wave of ideas from this concept

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: That Tesla method you could also have all motors located inside your home only a funnel located on the exterior wall. Bet your area doesnt have a regulation for that . I doubt anyone has a random funnel on their homes lmao

replies.author: @stevetobias4890

author: @michaeltucker8645
text: What happened to your cone? That sucker was spinning great. Fantastic job. I'd imagine with a cone it possibly turn a larger motor even.  I like this idea bc I rent my property and cant mount anything to my home like this but I do own my shed  on it and the roof is completely taken up with solar panels.  Instead of come out in front. Could you not take a squirrel cage type in closure  pointing the large opening out as the cone and the smaller towards the fan beside it on the wall of your home. Completely around all sides of it.  They could  be  fair small and you wouldnt need such a large fan blade , might even still use the original and motor. That would greatly reduce  the profile of it coming off your home if size in your area is a problem.

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Do you think in summer obviously the heat vent in some older homes that's in the middle of the tallest pitch of  the roof  with a funneling cone or cage box style could produce enough wind speed from the heat naturally venting out. Asking bc then you definitely would produce power even on days with little to no wind at all.

replies.author: @michaeltucker8645
replies.text: Again the ideas are flowing. Would  even be possible to take a funnel wind catcher and some pvc pipes a few feet long and put the funneled air outlet into your attic crawl space. Be better if your attic vent was on the side of the home and not out the top bc then using the cage fans style wind accelerator box to propel your generator fan. The cooler air coming in would push the hottest air out at a much faster rate then when it entered I'd imagen and the cage box increasing the speed upon exit even more so. Have to play around with the funnel size and cage box input and output diameters. Id assume if anyone would be willing to cut 2 holes for funnels they wouldnt mind  closing off a top vent exhaust system  and just cut a 3rd hole for the cage box as well.  The idea came from double down on the funnels ability to catch wind and cage to increase its speed  then quadruple down assuming bc your generator is producing all year around . In summer the cooler air coming in possible lighten load on your havc system  as well as help powering it at the same time.  I'm sure same method  could be used to in reverse in winter possibly with solar air heaters  from soda cans to heat the crawl space in winter keeping a constant wind on the generator 24/7 n any wind conditions.
